title: Quiz: Peephole Optimization
description: Test your understanding of local circuit optimization techniques

questions.question: What distinguishes peephole optimization from template matching?

options.text: Peephole is slower but more accurate
Peephole computes combined unitaries and resynthesizes, rather than matching fixed patterns
Template matching works on any gates, peephole only works on rotations
They are the same technique with different names

questions.explanation: Peephole optimization collects gates in a window, multiplies their matrices to get the combined effect, and resynthesizes to an optimal sequence. Template matching looks for predefined patterns. Peephole can optimize arbitrary sequences.

questions.question: What decomposition is typically used to resynthesize single-qubit gate sequences?
questions.explanation: ZYZ (or ZXZ) Euler decomposition expresses any single-qubit unitary as Rz(phi)*Ry(theta)*Rz(lambda) with at most 3 rotations, making it ideal for resynthesis.

questions.question: If a window of 5 single-qubit gates computes to the identity matrix, what should the peephole optimizer output?

options.text: An error, since this is invalid

questions.explanation: If the combined unitary is identity (up to global phase), the optimal resynthesis is no gates at all. The peephole optimizer should output an empty sequence, effectively removing all 5 gates.

questions.question: Peephole optimization always reduces gate count compared to the original circuit.
questions.explanation: Peephole optimization aims to reduce gate count but may sometimes produce the same count or even more gates (e.g., when decomposing to a restricted basis). It only applies changes when beneficial.

questions.question: For two-qubit peephole optimization, which decomposition is commonly used?
questions.explanation: KAK (or Cartan) decomposition expresses any two-qubit unitary in terms of single-qubit gates and a canonical two-qubit interaction, enabling optimal CNOT count resynthesis.

questions.question: Why is basis-aware peephole optimization important?

options.text: Hardware only supports specific native gates, and resynthesis should target those gates
It makes the optimizer run faster
It is only needed for error correction

questions.explanation: Real quantum hardware only supports specific native gates (e.g., IBMQ uses CX, RZ, SX). Basis-aware optimization resynthesizes to these native gates, avoiding additional transpilation overhead.
